hook: You already know that $2 + 2 = 4$ is true and $2 + 2 = 5$ is false — but mathematics demands far more precision than everyday language can provide. Without a shared, rigorous language, even a brilliant argument can collapse into ambiguity or be misread entirely. How do mathematicians ensure that every statement means exactly one thing, and that every proof is airtight?
concept: This unit builds the grammar and vocabulary of mathematics itself — the idea that before you can prove anything, you must be able to state it with absolute precision. Formally, this is the study of mathematical logic and notation as the foundation for all rigorous reasoning.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to distinguish precise mathematical statements from vague expressions and read standard notation fluently, so every claim you make has a definite truth value.
Master the symbols $\land$, $\lor$, $\forall$, $\exists$, and more to combine and quantify statements exactly as mathematicians do.
Apply De Morgan's laws and quantifier-negation rules to correctly negate any statement, including complex ones involving $\forall$ and $\exists$.
You will learn to interpret and distinguish $P \Rightarrow Q$, its converse, contrapositive, and biconditional $P \ Leftrightarrow Q$ — the logical backbone of nearly every theorem.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: How to Read and Write Mathematics
topic_roadmap.description: Develop the habits of reading proofs actively and writing arguments in clear, complete mathematical prose that a fellow mathematician can follow without ambiguity.
